_status: scaffold — LoRA not yet trained; rubric not yet authored. Production-ready target: week 2-3.

origin.region: Gond Adivasi community, primarily Madhya Pradesh (Mandla, Dindori, Patangarh), also Chhattisgarh, Maharashtra, Andhra Pradesh, Odisha
origin.antiquity: Wall-painting tradition documented for centuries on home interiors; modern paper-and-canvas form emerged in the 1980s through Jangarh Singh Shyam (1962-2001), inaugurating what is now called the Jangarh Kalam style
origin.primary_practitioners: Pardhan Gond community of Patangarh village, Mandla district, MP. Jangarh Singh Shyam (founder of the contemporary form); his widow Nankusia Shyam; Bhajju Shyam; Durgabai Vyam; Subhash Vyam; Venkat Raman Singh Shyam; the broader Patangarh artistic lineage.
origin.geocultural_landscape: The Pardhan Gond community historically served as bards/storytellers (Pardhan = 'one who proclaims') for the Raj Gond. The visual tradition is rooted in oral storytelling; each painting is a story-image.

visual_signature.line_discipline: Bold outline; interior filled with a tradition-specific PATTERN VOCABULARY (dots, dashes, fishscale, wave-lines, crescents, leaves, dotted-lines, hatched-lines)
visual_signature.color_strategy: Vibrant flat colors — bright vermillion, electric blue, emerald, magenta, yellow, orange; high-saturation; multi-color compositions
visual_signature.composition: Single subject typically dominant in frame; subject's body interior filled entirely with rhythmic pattern; background can be plain color OR populated with smaller secondary elements
visual_signature.ornament_density: Dense — every interior surface filled with pattern; the rhythmic pattern IS the aesthetic, more than color choice
visual_signature.background_strategy: Either solid flat color contrasting with the subject, OR populated with story-context elements (trees, smaller animals, narrative motifs)
visual_signature.negative_space_treatment: Minimal but acceptable — Gond does not require Madhubani-level fill; subject can sit on a colored ground
visual_signature.ground_line: Implied by composition; not a separate decorative element like Madhubani

border_grammars: typically borderless — frame is the rectangle of the canvas; some contemporary Gond uses a thin black border

prompt_scaffold.positive_prefix: Gond folk-art painting of {subject}, bold black outline, interior body filled with rhythmic Gond pattern vocabulary (dots, dashes, fishscale, crescents, wave-lines), vibrant multi-color flat-color regions, Jangarh Kalam style from Patangarh Madhya Pradesh, single subject prominent in frame
prompt_scaffold.negative_prefix: photorealistic, 3D render, photographic shading, perspective depth, Madhubani-style ornate borders, Warli-style two-color minimalism, vector clean lines, Disney style, anime style, anatomically realistic fur and feather detail, watercolor, oil paint, blank empty interior
hard_negatives_v0_1: no plain undecorated body interior — Gond REQUIRES interior pattern fill
no Madhubani-style border-grammar — Gond is typically borderless
no Warli-style two-color restraint — Gond is multi-color
no Madhubani-style ornate floral medallions — Gond patterns are RHYTHMIC (repeated dots/dashes/lines), not ornate motifs
interior pattern MUST be the Gond vocabulary (dots, dashes, fishscale, etc.), not generic AI-art noise
double-contour outlines are NOT Gond — single bold outline is correct
the rhythmic interior pattern is the defining feature — if it's absent, this is not Gond

production_recipe.caption_template: a gond folk-art painting of [SUBJECT], jangarh kalam style, pardhan gond tradition from patangarh madhya pradesh, bold outline, rhythmic interior pattern fill with [PATTERN VOCABULARY], vibrant multi-color
production_recipe._path_note: BRAND-LORA.md is at repo root; this style.json lives at brand/gond/

lora_training.reference_corpus_status: TO COLLECT: ~50 attributable Gond paintings — primary sources include Bhajju Shyam's published catalogues (Tara Books), MAP Bengaluru collection, Tribal Museum Bhopal, Jangarh Singh Shyam estate publications

qc_rubric._status: to_be_authored — distinct from Madhubani and Warli rubrics
planned_checks: interior-fill-coverage (subject body is patterned, not plain)
single-subject-prominence (subject dominates frame; not Warli-crowded multi-figure)

icon_64.notes: Bold silhouettes survive but interior pattern detail lost — recommend Warli for icons

print_a3.notes: Via RealESRGAN 3x upscale; pattern preserves well

gallery_a1.notes: Via RealESRGAN 8x; pattern is the strength of Gond at large size

cultural_attribution.tradition_owners: Pardhan Gond community of Patangarh village, Mandla district, Madhya Pradesh, India
cultural_attribution.named_lineage_acknowledgment: Jangarh Singh Shyam (1962-2001) — founder of the contemporary form (Jangarh Kalam); his lineage continues through Nankusia Shyam, Bhajju Shyam, Durgabai Vyam, Subhash Vyam, Venkat Raman Singh Shyam, and the broader Patangarh artistic lineage.
cultural_attribution.mandatory_attribution_text: This Gond-tradition artifact draws on the Jangarh Kalam contemporary form of Pardhan Gond folk-art, originating in Patangarh village, Mandla district, Madhya Pradesh, India. The form was inaugurated by Jangarh Singh Shyam (1962-2001) and is sustained today by his family and the broader Patangarh artistic lineage.
cultural_attribution.commercial_use_note: Commercial use should support Pardhan Gond artist cooperatives, the Jangarh Singh Shyam family lineage at Patangarh, and Adivasi cultural-preservation organizations. The tradition is community-rooted but has specific named lineage holders — both community AND lineage attribution apply.

novelty_handling.policy: Same as other traditions — novel subjects disclosed in receipt's `cultural_attribution.novelty_disclosure`.
novelty_handling.gond_specific_note: Gond has a strong tradition of depicting animals across categories (tiger, deer, elephant, fish, bird, snake, mythological creatures). The Jangarh Kalam form has been particularly open to subject expansion — Bhajju Shyam's 'The London Jungle Book' (2004) depicts London-via-Gond. The tradition's named lineage HAS authorized subject expansion in published work. Forge can extend Gond to global fauna with stronger precedent than Warli or Madhubani — but still discloses each novel-subject case in receipt metadata.
